title: Parameterized Property Projection
narrative:
A **parameterized projection lens** is a bidirectional mapping between a mutable
    source property of type `A` and a target type `B`, where the projection itself is
    shaped by a **read-only `Val` parameter** of type `P`. In the optics literature this
    is also called an *indexed lens* or a *dependent lens*: the "view" you get depends
    on an extra value that travels alongside the lens.

    Think of the parameter as the *perspective* or *context* through which the source
    is observed. A temperature in Celsius, viewed through the parameter "Fahrenheit",
    becomes a Fahrenheit number. The same temperature, viewed through "Kelvin", becomes
    something else. Writes through the projected view are inverted back to the source,
    but the parameter itself is **never modified** — it is strictly an input.

    This is different from the dual-source projection (`Var.of(first, second, getter, setter)`)
    where both sources are mutable and both receive write-back. A parameterized projection
    has one mutable source and one read-only parameter; writes flow to the source only.

    There are four factory variants for creating a parameterized projection:

    1. `source.projectTo(parameter, getter, setter)` — Non-nullable, type inferred
    2. `source.projectTo(Class, parameter, getter, setter)` — Non-nullable, explicit type
    3. `source.projectTo(nullObject, parameter, getter, setter)` — Non-nullable with fallback
    4. `source.projectToNullable(Class, parameter, getter, setter)` — Nullable

    This specification illustrates the feature through a parade of creative little
    scenarios — math, geography, ethics, animals — and verifies the full contract:
    forward and backward propagation, parameter-driven re-projection, identity flags,
    string representation, listener lifecycle, memory safety, and error handling.

features.id: A parameterized projection can round a number to any number of decimal places chosen by a parameter.

extraInfo:
A classical parameterized mapping in mathematics is **rounding**. The same raw number
            looks different depending on how many decimal places you ask for. Here the source
            holds the raw number, the parameter decides the precision, and the lens serves up
            a rounded view.

            When you write through the rounded view, the source is replaced by the rounded value
            (at the same precision) — a perfectly lawful round-trip for a fixed parameter.

blocks.text: A source property holding an unrounded number, and a parameter for the precision.
A parameterized projection that rounds the raw number using the precision parameter.
Initially the raw number is projected at two decimal places.
We change the parameter to show more decimal places.
The view updates even though the source has not changed.
We write a coarsely-rounded value back through the lens.
The source is replaced with the rounded value, the parameter is untouched.

features.id: When the parameter changes, the projected value is recomputed and observers are notified.

extraInfo:
The whole point of a parameterized projection is that changes to the parameter
            re-shape the view. Observers subscribed to the lens must see these re-projections
            as ordinary change events.

blocks.text: A source number and a parameter controlling how we bin it.
A projection computing "n mod divisor".
code: (Integer r, Integer d) -> r    // writing back just sets the source to `r`

blocks.text: A trace of every projected value we see.
The initial remainder is correct.
We change the divisor (the parameter).
The remainder is re-projected and an event is fired.
We change the source number.
The remainder updates again because the source changed.

features.id: A parameterized lens can convert a Euro amount into a chosen European currency.

extraInfo:
A Eurozone shop holds prices in euros but wants to display them in the visitor's
            preferred currency. The parameter carries the chosen currency (and its rate),
            the source holds the canonical Euro amount, and the lens produces the converted
            amount. Changing the currency re-projects the price instantly; entering a price
            in the foreign currency is inverted back into euros.

blocks.text: A Euro price and a target currency parameter.
A parameterized lens converting the price into the target currency.
In euros the displayed price equals the source.
We switch the display currency to Pounds.
The displayed amount is converted automatically, the source is untouched.
A user enters 430 Polish Złoty as the new price.
The source (in euros) is computed by inverting the current rate.
The currency parameter itself is NEVER written to by the lens.

features.id: A timezone-aware clock can be expressed as a parameterized lens over a UTC time.

extraInfo:
Stored internally as UTC, displayed in whatever offset the user selected: this
            everyday UI pattern is, in optics terms, a parameterized lens. The source is the
            canonical UTC time, the parameter is the chosen zone offset, and the lens yields
            the local `LocalTime` for that offset. Writes through the lens shift the local
            time back to UTC using the current offset.

blocks.text: A time stored in UTC and a selected offset (the parameter).
A parameterized lens yielding the local time for the chosen offset.
At UTC the local time equals the source.
We switch to Central European Time (UTC+1).
The local time shifts forward one hour; the UTC source is unchanged.
We write the local time as 16:30 while the offset is still UTC+1.
The UTC source shifts back by one hour.

features.id: A parameterized projection can evaluate an act under a chosen ethical framework.

extraInfo:
The same act — "a trolley that saves five people but harms one" — is judged very
            differently depending on the moral lens you wear. A utilitarian counts net benefit;
            a deontologist sees an impermissible act; a virtue ethicist looks at the character
            of the agent.

            Here the source is the *act* itself (as a record), and the parameter is the moral
            framework. The lens produces a concise verdict. The inverse is fuzzy — we choose
            a plausible act that a given framework would label with the same verdict. This is
            illustrative rather than lawful, and is a good example of where parameterization
            stops pretending to be a perfect isomorphism and simply becomes a useful view.

blocks.text: An act, and a framework parameter.
A parameterized projection rendering a verdict.
code: return a.peopleHarmed() > 0 ? "Impermissible (harm is used as a means)" : "Permissible"
return "Depends on character of the agent"
// Inverse: given a verdict and a framework, fabricate a plausible act.
// Writes through the lens are coarse — we pick a canonical representative.

blocks.text: Under utilitarianism, the act is net-positive.
We switch to a deontological lens.
The same act becomes impermissible — the verdict is re-projected.
code: verdict.get() == "Impermissible (harm is used as a means)"

blocks.text: The virtue ethicist is consulted.
The verdict shifts to the classical non-committal answer.
code: verdict.get() == "Depends on character of the agent"

blocks.text: We write a "Permissible" verdict under deontology.
The inverse chooses a canonical permissible act.

features.id: A parameterized lens can express an animal weight in species-appropriate units.

extraInfo:
A zookeeper records the raw mass of every animal in grams, but the display on the
            enclosure sign should use units appropriate for the species: grams for a mouse,
            kilograms for a cat or a wolf, pounds for visitors from the US perhaps. A
            parameterized projection cleanly separates *what the animal actually weighs* (the
            source) from *how we choose to express it* (the parameter).

blocks.text: A mass in grams and a chosen display unit.
A parameterized projection converting grams into the chosen unit.
The cats weight is shown in kilograms.
We switch to grams for a mouse-sized creature on another screen.
The same mass is now shown in grams; the source was never written.
A visitor types 10 pounds as the new weight.
The source stores the equivalent mass in grams, the unit is untouched.

features.id: The explicit-type variant of projectTo enforces the declared class.

extraInfo:
When the projected type is polymorphic (interface, abstract class) the inferred
            type would be the concrete (sub)class of the first computed value, which is a trap
            for later subclasses. The explicit-type overload fixes the declared type up front.

blocks.text: A numeric source and a parameter.
A parameterized projection with an explicit Number type.
The declared type is the polymorphic Number class.
We write a Double through the view — subtype of Number.
The source accepts the inverted result.

features.id: The fallback variant of projectTo returns a configured value when the source is null.

extraInfo:
When the source is nullable and might hold null, the fallback overload wraps the
            getter so that a null source is safely mapped to a pre-configured "null object"
            value. This keeps the projected property non-nullable and easy to consume.

blocks.text: A nullable source and a parameter controlling the greeting formality.
A parameterized projection with a fallback greeting.
When the source is null, the fallback is used.
We set the source to an actual name.
The getter runs normally and produces the formal greeting.
We switch to informal mode.
The projection is recomputed, the source stays the same.

features.id: The nullable variant of projectToNullable allows null to flow through the view.

extraInfo:
Sometimes a parameterized conversion is inherently partial: parsing a localized
            number, looking up a country-specific greeting, etc. The nullable overload makes
            the resulting property admit null, so that the getter can gracefully signal
            "no result" without throwing.

blocks.text: A source holding a species enum and a parameter deciding whether to label it.
A nullable projection that returns a label when enabled, null otherwise.
code: (Boolean enabled, Species s) -> enabled ? ("Species: " + s.name()) : null,

blocks.text: Initially a label is produced.
We disable labeling.
The view yields null.
We assign a concrete label while labeling is on.
The source is updated to the parsed species.

features.id: A parameterized lens reports itself as mutable and a lens, not a view.

extraInfo:
A parameterized projection is a **mutable** property — you can write through it —
            and a **lens**, because it sits over other properties. It is not a view
            (views are strictly read-only).

blocks.text: Some trivial parameterized projection.
code: var lens = src.projectTo(par, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x * p, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v.intdiv(p))

features.id: Nullability flags match the chosen factory variant.

code: var nonNull      = src.projectTo(par, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x + p, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v - p)
var withFallback = src.projectTo(0, par, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x + p, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v - p)
var nullable     = src.projectToNullable(Integer.class, par, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x + p, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v - p)

features.id: A parameterized lens has a "ParamLens" string representation carrying the type and value.

blocks.text: An integer source and a parameter controlling how many times to repeat it.
A parameterized projection that multiplies n by count.
The string form identifies the core as a ParamLens.

features.id: A parameterized lens with a custom id includes the id in its string form.

blocks.text: A source and a parameter, and a named parameterized lens.

features.id: A nullable parameterized lens uses a "?" suffix on its type.

code: (Boolean enabled, String s) -> enabled ? s.toUpperCase() : null,
(String v, Boolean enabled) -> v == null ? "" : v.toLowerCase()

features.id: Observers on a parameterized lens are notified both by source and parameter changes.

extraInfo:
A parameterized lens listens on both the source and the parameter, so either one
            can cause a change event to fire on the lens. Observers see the projected value
            after each update.

blocks.text: A source, a parameter, and a parameterized lens.
code: (WeightUnit u, Double g) -> u == WeightUnit.GRAMS ? g : g / 1000.0d,
(Double v, WeightUnit u) -> u == WeightUnit.GRAMS ? v : v * 1000.0d

blocks.text: A trace of all projected values seen.
We change the parameter.
An event fires for the re-projection.
We change the source.
Another event fires.
We write back through the lens.
A further event fires with the value we set.

features.id: Observers can be unsubscribed from a parameterized lens.

blocks.text: A parameterized lens and a tracing observer.
code: var lens = src.projectTo(Integer.class, par, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x + p, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v - p)

blocks.text: We change the source.
We unsubscribe and change again.
No further notifications reach the observer.

features.id: numberOfChangeListeners reflects registrations and unsubscribeAll clears them.

code: var lens = src.projectTo(par, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x + p, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v - p)

features.id: The channel on which a write happens is preserved through the lens.

extraInfo:
Channels (`From.VIEW_MODEL`, `From.VIEW`, `From.ALL`) are threaded through every
            change event. A change fired on a specific channel should reach only the observers
            that listen on that channel (plus `From.ALL`).

blocks.text: A parameterized lens.
Traces per channel.
The source is updated on the VIEW_MODEL channel.
The projected property is written on the VIEW channel.

features.id: Writes through the lens never modify the parameter, only the source.

extraInfo:
This is the defining property of a parameterized projection: the parameter is a
            read-only input. No matter how we write through the lens, the parameter retains
            exactly the value we handed in.

blocks.text: A parameter and a source.
A parameterized projection computing "euros * rate" as the view.
An observer on the parameter that should never fire as a result of lens writes.
We write through the projected view.
The source is updated with the inverted value, the parameter is untouched.
No event is fired on the parameter.

features.id: Writing the same projected value does not fire a redundant change event.

code: var lens = src.projectTo(Integer.class, par, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x + p, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v - p)

blocks.text: We write the same current value through the lens.
No event is fired because the projected value did not change.

features.id: A parameterized lens returns the last known value when the getter throws.

extraInfo:
If the getter throws a (non-fatal) exception while recomputing the projected value,
            the lens logs the failure and falls back to its last successfully computed value
            rather than propagating the exception.

blocks.text: A source, a parameter, and a flag we can flip to make the getter fail.
The initial value is correct.
We make the getter fail and change the source.
No exception escapes to the caller.
The lens serves the last known value.

features.id: A parameterized lens handles a failing setter gracefully, leaving the source untouched.

blocks.text: We flip the fail flag and try to write.
No exception is thrown.
The source is unchanged.

features.id: A non-null parameterized lens whose getter returns null on first call throws immediately.

blocks.text: We try to build a non-nullable parameterized lens whose getter yields null.
A NullPointerException is thrown at construction time.

features.id: Factories reject null arguments up front.

blocks.text: null parameter
code: src.projectTo((Val<Integer>) null, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v)

blocks.text: null getter
code: src.projectTo(par, (java.util.function.BiFunction<Integer, Integer, Integer>) null, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v)

blocks.text: null setter
code: src.projectTo(par, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x, (java.util.function.BiFunction<Integer, Integer, Integer>) null)

features.id: withId produces a copy of the lens that continues to receive source and parameter updates.

blocks.text: We change the source through the original property.
The renamed copy reflects the update too.

features.id: A parameterized lens is garbage collected when no longer referenced, freeing listeners on source AND parameter.

extraInfo:
The lens registers itself as a weak listener on both the source and the parameter.
            When the only strong reference to the lens is dropped, the lens becomes eligible
            for garbage collection and both listener slots are cleaned up automatically.

blocks.text: A source and a parameter.
No listeners initially.
We create a parameterized lens and only hold it locally.
code: var lens = src.projectTo(Integer.class, par, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x + p, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v - p)

blocks.text: Each of the source and parameter has a single change listener.
We drop the strong reference and force GC.
The lens is collected and no listeners remain on either.

features.id: A parameterized lens holds strong references to its source and parameter, preventing their GC.

extraInfo:
A parameterized lens must be able to read the source and parameter whenever it is
            asked for the projected value, so it keeps both alive. Releasing outside references
            to them does not collect them as long as the lens itself is referenced.

code: var lens = src.projectTo(Integer.class, par, (Integer p, Integer x) -> x + p, (Integer v, Integer p) -> v - p)

blocks.text: We drop outside references to source and parameter.
Neither is collected; the lens still works.

features.id: Parameterized lenses compose: a weight in grams can be shown in any unit AND rounded at a chosen precision.

extraInfo:
Because a parameterized lens is itself a `Var`, we can stack one on top of another:
            first convert from grams to a chosen unit, then round the result to a chosen
            precision. The two parameters together steer a clean, human-readable display.
            This is the reactive analogue of function composition.

blocks.text: A mass in grams and two parameters — display unit and precision.
First projection: grams → unit-of-choice.
Second projection stacked on top: round to the chosen number of decimal places.
10 lbs shown in pounds and rounded to 2 places is exactly 10.00.
We change the precision to 4.
The display refines, the source is untouched.
We switch the unit to kilograms.
The displayed value is recomputed end-to-end.
